content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_007_page_001.jpg

1. And concerning the [things] about which you wrote: "It is good for a man not to touch a woman."

2. But because of the sexual immoralities, let each [man] have the own wife, and let each [woman] have the own husband.

3. Let the husband render to the wife the obligation, and likewise also the wife to the husband.

4. The wife does not have authority over the own body, but the husband; and likewise also the husband does not have authority over the own body, but the wife.

5. Do not deprive one another, except by agreement for a time, that you may devote [yourselves] to prayer, and again be together, lest the Satan tempt you because of the lack of self-control of you.

6. And this I say as a concession, not as a command.

7. For I wish all men to be as I myself [am]; but each has [his] own gift from God, one of this [kind] and one of that [kind].

8. And I say to the unmarried and to the widows: it is good for them if they remain as I [am].

9. But if they do not have self-control, let them marry; for it is better to marry than to burn.

10. And to the [ones] having married I charge — not I but the Lord — a wife is not to separate from [the] husband

11. (but if she does separate, let her remain unmarried or be reconciled to the husband), and a husband is not to divorce [the] wife.

12. And to the rest I say — I, not the Lord — if any brother has an unbelieving wife, and she consents to dwell with him, let him not divorce her.

13. And a woman who has an unbelieving husband, and he consents to dwell with her, let her not divorce the husband.

14. For the unbelieving husband has been sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife has been sanctified by the brother; since otherwise the children of you are unclean, but now they are holy.

15. But if the unbelieving [one] separates, let [him/her] separate. The brother or the sister is not enslaved in such [cases]. But in peace God has called you.

16. For how do you know, wife, whether you will save the husband? Or how do you know, husband, whether you will save the wife?

17. Only, as the Lord has assigned to each, as God has called each, thus let him walk. And thus I direct in all the assemblies.

18. Was anyone called [while] circumcised? Let him not become uncircumcised. Was anyone called in uncircumcision? Let him not be circumcised.

19. The circumcision is nothing, and the uncircumcision is nothing, but [the] keeping of [the] commandments of God.

20. Each in the calling in which he was called, in this let him remain.

21. Were you called a slave? Let it not be a concern to you. But if indeed you are able to become free, rather make use [of it].

22. For the [one] called in [the] Lord [as] a slave is a freedperson of [the] Lord; likewise the [one] called [as] free is a slave of Christos.

23. You were bought with a price; do not become slaves of men.

24. Each [one], brothers, in that in which he was called, in this let him remain with God.

25. And concerning the virgins, a command of [the] Lord I do not have, but a judgment I give as one having received mercy from [the] Lord to be faithful.

26. I consider then this to be good because of the present distress — that it is good for a man to be thus.

27. Have you been bound to a wife? Do not seek release. Have you been released from a wife? Do not seek a wife.

28. But if indeed you marry, you did not sin; and if the virgin marries, she did not sin. But affliction in the flesh such [ones] will have, and I spare you.

29. And this I say, brothers: the time has been shortened. For the rest, that both the [ones] having wives may be as not having,

30. and the [ones] weeping as not weeping, and the [ones] rejoicing as not rejoicing, and the [ones] buying as not possessing,

31. and the [ones] using the world as not fully using [it]. For the form of this world is passing away.

32. And I want you to be free from anxiety. The unmarried [man] is anxious about the [things] of the Lord, how he may please the Lord.

33. But the [one] having married is anxious about the [things] of the world, how he may please the wife,

34. and he is divided. And the unmarried woman and the virgin is anxious about the [things] of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit. But the [one] having married is anxious about the [things] of the world, how she may please the husband.

35. And this I say for the benefit of you yourselves, not that I may cast a restraint upon you, but for the seemly and devoted [attention] to the Lord without distraction.

36. And if anyone thinks he is behaving improperly toward the virgin of him, if she is past the bloom [of youth], and thus it ought to be, let him do what he wishes; he does not sin. Let them marry.

37. But the [one] who stands firm in the heart of him, not having necessity, but has authority over the own will, and has judged this in the own heart, to keep the own virgin, he will do well.

38. So then, both the [one] giving in marriage does well, and the [one] not giving in marriage will do better.

39. A wife is bound for as long a time as the husband of her lives; but if the husband falls asleep, she is free to be married to whom she wishes, only in [the] Lord.

40. But she is more blessed if she remains thus, according to the judgment of me; and I think I also have [the] Spirit of God.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "It is good for a man not to touch a woman":** This is likely a Corinthian slogan that Paul is quoting, not his own view. The quotation marks are editorial. Some Corinthians, in reaction to the immorality of ch.5-6, had swung to an ascetic extreme.

2. **v.10 — "I, not the Lord" / v.12 — "I, not the Lord":** Paul distinguishes between direct commands of Jesus (v.10, cf. Mark 10:11-12) and his own apostolic judgment (v.12). This distinction is unique in Paul's letters and reveals his care for the authority behind his instructions.

3. **v.15 — "The Pauline privilege":** In later church law, this verse became the basis for the "Pauline privilege" — the dissolution of a marriage between a believer and an unbeliever when the unbeliever departs.

4. **v.21 — Ambiguous directive:** μᾶλλον χρῆσαι — "rather make use [of it]." The ambiguity is whether "it" refers to the opportunity for freedom (become free) or the current state of slavery (use your slavery for God). Most interpreters favor the former.

5. **v.29 — "the time has been shortened":** ὁ καιρὸς συνεσταλμένος ἐστίν — Paul's eschatological urgency. The "as if not" (ὡς μή) passages (vv.29-31) express an eschatological relativization of all earthly statuses and activities.

6. **vv.36-38 — The "virgin" passage:** Highly debated. The situation may involve: (a) a father deciding about his daughter's marriage, (b) a man and his fiancée, or (c) a spiritual marriage (celibate couple). Option (b) is most likely.

— EMET, AI Translator
